🟥 THE IRAN WAR JUST CROSSED INTO CIVILIAN INFRASTRUCTURE AND SAUDI ARABIA IS COUNTING ITS FIRST DEAD

Bahrain said an Iranian drone damaged a desalination plant on Sunday.

That is the first confirmed attack on a water treatment facility by any side in this war. Desalination plants supply drinking water to millions of people across the Gulf states, including hundreds of thousands of Filipino workers.

The strike on the Bahrain facility did not knock the water supply offline, according to the country's water authority. But the precedent it sets is what drew immediate alarm from regional governments and international observers.

Hours earlier, Iran accused the United States of striking a desalination plant on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz, cutting water to 30 villages. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Washington "set this precedent, not Iran." US Central Command spokesperson Captain Tim Hawkins responded: "U.S. forces do not target civilians, period."

Both sides are now accusing each other of attacking civilian water infrastructure. The distinction between military and civilian targets has eroded.

🟥 SAUDI ARABIA'S FIRST DEATHS

Saudi Arabia reported its first fatalities from the conflict on Sunday.

A military projectile struck a residential area, killing two people, one Indian national and one Bangladeshi national, according to the Saudi defense ministry. Twelve other Bangladeshi workers were wounded.

Foreign workers have made up the majority of casualties in Gulf states since the war began on February 28. In Israel, Filipino caregiver Mary Ann de Vera, 32, from Pangasinan, was killed by shrapnel from an Iranian missile in Tel Aviv on the first day of the war.

Bahrain, Kuwait, and the UAE reported additional Iranian missile and drone launches toward their territories on Sunday. The UAE said Iran had launched more than 100 missiles and drones in new barrages. Only four drones landed on Emirati soil, according to its defense ministry.

Kuwait intercepted multiple attacks. Qatar intercepted a ballistic missile on March 7. Bahrain, which hosts the US Navy's Fifth Fleet, has seen strikes on hotels, ports, and residential towers.

Since February 28, at least 18 people have been killed in Gulf states, 10 of them civilians, according to an AFP tally, in addition to at least 11 dead in Israel and more than 1,230 in Iran.

🟥 IRAN REVERSES COURSE ON APOLOGY

On Saturday, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian had offered an apology to Gulf neighbors, expressing regret for Iranian strikes on their soil. By Sunday, he had reversed himself.

"When we are attacked, we have no choice but to respond. The more pressure they impose on us, the stronger our response will naturally be," Pezeshkian said in a video statement. "Our Iran will not bow easily in the face of bullying, oppression, or aggression."

Judiciary chief Gholam Hossein Mohseni-Ejei had already contradicted the Saturday apology on X: "The geography of some countries in the region is in the hands of the enemy, and those points are used against our country. Intense attacks on these targets will continue."

Pezeshkian and Mohseni-Ejei sit on the three-member leadership council that has governed Iran since the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in a strike on February 28.

🟥 WHAT BURNED IN TEHRAN

Israeli warplanes struck four oil storage tankers and a petroleum transfer terminal in Tehran overnight Saturday, killing four people, according to Iranian officials. Witnesses said the smoke was so dense it felt like the sun had not risen.

The Iranian Red Crescent Society said approximately 10,000 civilian structures across Iran have been damaged since the war began, including homes, schools, and nearly three dozen health facilities. It warned Tehran residents of toxic air pollution and the risk of acid rain.

Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf warned Sunday that the war's impact on Iran's oil industry would continue to spiral, saying it could soon become harder to produce and sell oil.

🟥 WHAT THIS MEANS FOR FILIPINOS IN THE GULF

There are approximately 2.4 million Filipinos living and working in the Middle East, according to the Department of Foreign Affairs. At least 1,979 OFWs in nine Gulf countries have requested repatriation as of last week.

The desalination plant attacks are a direct concern. Gulf states rely on these facilities for potable water. A sustained campaign against water infrastructure would threaten the basic survival of civilian populations, including migrant workers.

Airspace closures continue to delay repatriation flights. Dubai reopened briefly, allowing 299 Filipinos to return on March 6, but most Middle Eastern airspace remains restricted.

The Department of Migrant Workers on Saturday declared the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas," allowing Filipino seafarers to legally refuse to sail those routes.

🟥 THE SUPREME COURT JUST JOINED THE 4-DAY WORKWEEK AND NOW ALL THREE BRANCHES OF GOVERNMENT ARE ON ENERGY CONSERVATION MODE

The judiciary did not wait long.

One day after President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. ordered a temporary four-day onsite work schedule for the executive branch, the Supreme Court issued its own memorandum circular. Starting Monday, March 9, all SC employees will follow a four-day onsite, one-day work-from-home arrangement.

First-level and second-level courts will do the same. Odd-numbered branches report onsite Monday through Thursday with Friday as a WFH day. Even-numbered branches report Tuesday through Friday with Monday off. The schedules alternate weekly, according to GMA News.

The legislature had already shifted to compressed schedules before the executive branch acted. Local government units across the country have also moved to abbreviated work weeks. With the judiciary now in, all three branches of the Philippine government are operating on reduced schedules for the first time in response to a single energy crisis.

🟥 THE REASON IS FUEL

The trigger is the Iran war.

Marcos announced the executive branch shift on Friday, March 7, framing it as an energy conservation measure. Memorandum Circular No. 114 instructed all executive departments and agencies to cut electricity and fuel consumption by 10 to 20 percent. Non-essential government travel was banned. Meetings that could be held within offices were to replace external events.

The order exempted emergency and essential services, including police, firefighters, and frontline health workers.

Marcos was explicit about the cause. Global oil prices have surged because of the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran, which began on February 28 and has effectively shut down tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East.

The four-day arrangement remains in effect "until lifted or sooner revoked by the President," according to the circular.

🟥 LGUS WERE ALREADY MOVING

Several local governments did not wait for Manila.

The Department of Trade and Industry shifted its Makati offices to a remote work setup starting March 13, citing the anticipated impact of Middle East fuel prices, according to the Inquirer.

Multiple LGUs across the country began implementing compressed work weeks in early March. The Inquirer reported on March 9 that the shift has now spread to regional and municipal government offices nationwide.

The Civil Service Commission had previously issued guidelines under Memorandum Circular No. 6 (s. 2022), modified by MC No. 1 (s. 2025), allowing flexible work arrangements. Marcos's new circular builds on that existing framework.

🟥 LABOR GROUPS AND BUSINESS ARE PUSHING BACK

The four-day week is not without opposition.

The Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry warned on March 5 that a blanket implementation could "cripple output" in the country's industrial heartland, according to Manila Bulletin. PCCI president Perry Ferrer called for a "balanced and evidence-based" approach.

Ferrer argued that manufacturing, logistics, and export-dependent industries cannot simply compress their schedules without missing deadlines or breaching international delivery commitments. He pushed for a sector-sensitive framework with exemptions for industries that require 24/7 or six-day operations.

Labor groups also opposed the arrangement but for a different reason. Militant organizations described the scheme as "antiworker and antifamily," according to the Inquirer, arguing that compressed schedules increase daily work hours and commuting burdens without addressing the root cause of the crisis: fuel prices that workers cannot afford.

🟥 WHAT IT MEANS FOR FILIPINOS GOING TO COURT

The judiciary's entry is the most practically significant shift.

Filipinos with pending cases, scheduled hearings, or legal deadlines must now navigate an alternating schedule. A case assigned to an odd-numbered branch will find that court closed on Fridays one week and Mondays the next.

The SC circular did not specify how this affects case filing deadlines, though the alternating system is designed to ensure that at least half of all courts remain open on any given weekday.

For the millions of Filipinos who interact with courts for everything from land titles to annulments, from labor disputes to criminal complaints, the compressed schedule adds a new layer of scheduling complexity to an already strained judicial system.

🟥 THE SIGNAL

A four-day government work week is not a policy preference. When every branch of government simultaneously decides it can no longer afford to keep the lights on five days a week, that is a signal about the depth of the crisis.

Oil above $100 a barrel. A war with no end date. Fuel hikes projected to exceed P20 per liter on diesel by Tuesday. Emergency powers under negotiation. And now a government that has decided the best immediate response is to use less energy by working fewer days.

The arrangement is temporary. The question is how long temporary lasts.

🟥 CRUDE OIL JUST CROSSED $100 A BARREL AND THE BIGGEST FUEL PRICE HIKE IN PHILIPPINE HISTORY IS COMING ON TUESDAY

For nine days, the numbers climbed. Then they broke through.

Brent crude oil has surpassed $100 per barrel for the first time since the early days of the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, driven by a military conflict that shows no signs of slowing.

On Monday, the Inquirer reported that global benchmark crude had crossed the triple-digit threshold as the US-Israeli offensive against Iran entered its tenth day. Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow channel between Iran and Oman that carries roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply, has collapsed to near zero, according to Reuters vessel tracking data from March 6. That chokepoint normally handles 15 million barrels of crude per day.

The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East. Every Filipino who drives to work, rides a jeepney, or buys food trucked in from the provinces will feel this at the pump on Tuesday.

🟥 WHAT HITS ON TUESDAY

An industry source told the Inquirer on March 7 that diesel prices could surge by P20.10 to P20.30 per liter when the weekly fuel adjustment takes effect on March 10.

Gasoline is projected to spike by P10 to P10.20 per liter.

If these estimates hold, this would be the largest single-week increase in Philippine fuel prices in recent memory. Diesel, which was already climbing steeply, could approach P80 to P90 per liter, according to Philstar.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin told reporters that some fuel companies had already begun rolling out preemptive hikes of P0.50 to P5 per liter to avoid one massive adjustment, according to ABS-CBN. She also warned the public against panic-buying or hoarding, saying it could "disrupt" the market. The government maintains the country has a two-month fuel supply.

🟥 THE STRAIT THAT CONTROLS EVERYTHING

The Strait of Hormuz was not formally blockaded. Iran never declared it closed. But insurers withdrew coverage, ship owners pulled back, and the Revolutionary Guard Corps warned it would attack any vessel attempting to cross. By Wednesday, March 5, tanker crossings had dropped to zero, according to J.P. Morgan.

Wood Mackenzie called it a "dual supply shock." Exports were halted and OPEC's spare capacity was trapped behind the closed strait.

Saudi Arabia can redirect some volume via its East-West pipeline. The UAE has the Abu Dhabi pipeline. Together, roughly 6.5 million barrels per day, according to Rystad Energy. The strait normally carries more than double that.

Goldman Sachs estimated an $18-per-barrel risk premium already priced in. Barclays raised its Brent forecast to $100. Bloomberg reported traders warning that $100 was "imminent" if fighting continued.

🟥 THE BSP PROBLEM

BSP Governor Eli Remolona warned last week that the bank may have to raise interest rates if oil prices stayed above $90, according to the Inquirer.

February inflation came in at 2.4 percent. ING Bank analyst Deepali Bhargava warned that a sustained 20 percent increase in oil could add 0.5 percentage points to headline inflation. Capital Economics projected that oil above $80 per barrel could add 50 basis points. With oil now above $100, the math becomes even more punishing.

The peso is under pressure too. MUFG projected the currency could slide to P59 against the dollar if crude holds at $90. At $100, the picture worsens. A weaker peso amplifies the inflation impact by raising the cost of imported crude in local currency terms.

The BSP had been on a rate-cutting path. That path is now in question. BPI senior economist Jun Neri cautioned that inflation could approach the upper limit of the BSP target range, and that the central bank may hold rates rather than cut.

🟥 THE EMERGENCY POWERS PUSH

Congress is moving.

President Marcos told reporters on March 4 that he would seek emergency powers from Congress to reduce excise taxes on fuel products if Dubai crude stays above $80 per barrel. House Ways and Means Committee chairman Miro Quimbo filed House Bill 8257, which would give the President authority to suspend or lower petroleum excise during economic crises.

Separately, Navotas Representative Toby Tiangco proposed Joint House Resolution 4, calling for a halt in VAT collection on fuels during the crisis.

Senator Bam Aquino pointed out that the TRAIN law already has a provision for automatic suspension of fuel excise taxes when global oil prices exceed $80 per barrel for three consecutive months. But Palace Press Officer Claire Castro clarified that the TRAIN provision expired in 2020 and new legislation is needed.

Jetti Petroleum president Leo Bellas estimated that if the entire excise tax were suspended, diesel prices could drop by P6 per liter and gasoline by P10 per liter. The VAT component would further reduce the impact.

On Saturday, Senator Jan Almario Chan echoed the urgency, joining calls from both chambers for immediate action, according to Manila Bulletin.

🟥 WHAT COMES NEXT

The Department of Energy has instructed fuel companies to implement gradual price adjustments rather than a single massive spike. But "gradual" has limits when the underlying commodity is rising this fast.

Filipino households that spend disproportionately on transportation and food, two categories deeply sensitive to fuel costs, will feel this immediately. Food prices track diesel costs because trucks and fishing boats run on diesel.

The Bangko Sentral is watching. Congress is scrambling. The Palace is negotiating emergency powers.

And the war is still in its second week.

🟥 THE CENTRAL BANK JUST WARNED IT MAY HAVE TO RAISE INTEREST RATES IF OIL HITS $100

Six months of monetary easing could be about to reverse.

In a CNBC interview over the weekend, Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas Governor Eli Remolona Jr. said the central bank might need to shift gears and raise interest rates if global crude oil prices climb to $100 per barrel and the US dollar keeps strengthening against the peso.

Neither scenario is hypothetical anymore. Oil prices surged 27 percent in a single week after US-Israel strikes on Iran paralyzed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. The peso weakened to P58.44 against the dollar. February inflation jumped to a 13-month high of 2.4 percent.

The BSP has cut rates six times since August 2024, bringing its key policy rate down 225 basis points to a three-year low of 4.25 percent. That easing cycle was built to support an economy still dealing with the flood control corruption scandal and slow government spending.

Now the central bank faces a problem it did not expect this soon.

🟥 NOMURA PULLS ITS RATE CUT FORECAST

Japanese investment bank Nomura has revised its Philippine inflation forecast for 2026 upward to 3.2 percent, from 2.5 percent.

More significantly, Nomura has retracted the final 25-basis-point rate cut it had predicted for April, now expecting the BSP to hold its policy rate steady for the foreseeable future.

"Although the BSP had previously indicated the possibility of further easing after implementing a 25-basis-point cut in February, it is evident that future decisions will be data-driven, particularly concerning inflation," Nomura stated.

Capital Economics, meanwhile, projects that crude oil above $80 per barrel could add 50 basis points to headline inflation. ING Bank analysts warned that a price shock "of this magnitude," combined with peso depreciation, could push inflation to the upper end of the BSP's 2 to 4 percent target range.

BPI lead economist Emilio Neri Jr. warned inflation could reach 4 percent in coming months, noting that higher oil prices would compound existing rice-driven pressures and produce second-round effects across transport, electricity, and logistics.

🟥 THE REMITTANCE PROBLEM

Remolona also raised a concern that has received less attention than oil prices: the risk to overseas worker remittances.

Cash remittances from the Middle East totaled $6.5 billion in 2025, according to BSP data, roughly 18 percent of the Philippines' $35.6 billion total. With more than 2.4 million Filipinos working in the region, any sustained disruption to employment or income flows would be felt across the economy.

"There are some downside risks regarding the demand for our labor services. We are a significant exporter of labor services," Remolona told CNBC.

"While this is a concern, our top priority regarding Filipinos in the Middle East is to ensure their safety and potentially facilitate their return home."

Remolona called the Middle East remittance figure "very, very significant."

🟥 A THREE-WAY COLLISION

The BSP now faces a convergence of pressures that work against each other.

Oil prices are rising, which pushes inflation up. The peso is weakening, which makes imported oil even more expensive. Remittance flows face disruption, which would further weaken the peso and reduce household income.

Raising rates would slow an already underperforming economy. GDP growth was 4.2 percent in 2025, well below the government's 5.5 to 6 percent target.

The import dependency makes it worse. The Philippines sources roughly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East. Every 10 percent increase in oil prices adds an estimated 40 basis points to inflation and widens the current account deficit by about 60 basis points.

The Strait of Hormuz, where roughly 20 percent of global oil supply normally transits, has been effectively shut down since the conflict began on February 28. Iraq's oil production has collapsed by 70 percent. Kuwait declared force majeure. Qatar halted LNG production. Goldman Sachs raised its Brent crude forecast by $10 to $76 per barrel.

The Philippines does not produce enough of its own energy to cushion any of this.

🟥 THE HOUSEHOLD SQUEEZE

If the BSP is forced to pause or reverse its easing cycle because of an external oil shock, the ripple effects are immediate. Loan rates stop falling. Business expansion slows. The peso stays under pressure.

For ordinary Filipino households, it would mean higher borrowing costs on top of already record fuel prices.

The fuel price adjustment expected on March 10 is projected to be the largest single-day increase in Philippine history. DOE projections put diesel at P22 to P24 per liter higher, gasoline at P11 to P13, kerosene at P33 to P36. Those are increases, not new prices.

The BSP cut rates specifically to support an economy weakened by the flood control corruption scandal and sluggish government spending. GDP growth came in at 4.2 percent in 2025 — well below target.

Whether the central bank still has room to keep easing, or whether the war in the Middle East has closed that door, depends on what oil does next.

🟥 IRAN'S ASSEMBLY OF EXPERTS SAYS IT HAS CHOSEN A NEW SUPREME LEADER AS THE WAR ENTERS DAY 10

The vote has been cast.

Iran's Assembly of Experts, the 88-member clerical body constitutionally tasked with selecting the country's supreme leader, announced on Sunday that it has reached a decision on who will succeed the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.

The name has not been formally announced. But the clues are not subtle.

"The name of Khamenei will continue," Assembly member Hosseinali Eshkevari said in a video carried by Iranian media. "With the majority of votes, the person who will continue the path of Imam Khomeini and the path of the martyr Imam Khamenei has been chosen. The vote has been cast and will be announced soon."

Another member, Mohsen Heydari, told Iran's ISNA news agency that "the most suitable candidate, approved by the majority of the Assembly of Experts, has been determined."

Ahmad Alamolhoda, a senior cleric, went further. He said all reports suggesting the assembly had not yet decided were "completely false," adding that the head of the assembly's secretariat, Ayatollah Hashem Hosseini Bushehri, is now responsible for making the announcement public, according to Iran's Mehr news agency.

🟥 THE STRONGEST SIGNAL YET

A separate Assembly member, Ayatollah Mohsen Heidari Alekasir, offered what may be the most telling statement. He said the chosen successor was picked based on the late supreme leader's own criteria: that Iran's top leader should "be hated by the enemy" rather than admired by it.

"Even the Great Satan has mentioned his name," Heidari Alekasir said, according to Al Jazeera.

That remark came days after US President Donald Trump told reporters that Mojtaba Khamenei, the second son of the assassinated leader, was the most likely successor. Trump called it an "unacceptable" outcome and said he wanted to be personally involved in choosing Iran's next leader, according to a report by Axios.

Mojtaba Khamenei, a mid-ranking hardline cleric who has never held elected office, has long been considered the frontrunner. Iran International, citing informed sources, reported that the Revolutionary Guards had pushed for his appointment to be finalized quickly, even outside the legally prescribed procedures, given the ongoing airstrikes that have made a physical meeting of the assembly impossible.

🟥 THE DELAY

The formal announcement has been held up by a procedural dispute and a security calculation.

Some Assembly members believe the decision requires an in-person gathering. Others argue that meeting physically is too dangerous while American and Israeli warplanes continue to operate freely over Iranian airspace. Israel struck the building where the Assembly of Experts was meeting on Tuesday, according to Israeli sources who spoke to the Jerusalem Post.

The security fear runs deeper than procedure. The New York Times reported, citing unnamed officials, that the announcement has been delayed because Iranian authorities believe the new leader will be immediately targeted by Israel once his identity is confirmed.

Israel has given those fears weight. A spokesperson for the Israeli military said on Sunday that Israel would "pursue every successor" to Khamenei, according to multiple international outlets.

🟥 THE WAR ON DAY 10

The leadership announcement comes as the US-Israeli air campaign enters its tenth day with no sign of slowing.

According to Al Jazeera, citing Iran's health ministry, more than 1,300 people have been killed in Iran since February 28. Israel said over the weekend that it had struck 300 targets in Iran. CBS News reported that since the war began, Israel has conducted approximately 3,400 strikes and dropped roughly 7,500 munitions.

On Saturday, the air campaign escalated to oil infrastructure for the first time, according to Iran's Fars News Agency, with strikes on four oil storage facilities and one oil production transfer center in and around Tehran. On Sunday, Israeli strikes reportedly targeted an oil depot near Qeshm Airport in southern Iran, close to the Strait of Hormuz itself, according to social media reports that were corroborated by satellite imagery circulated by open-source intelligence analysts.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil passes, remains largely blocked. Reuters reported on Friday that tanker crossings had dropped to zero, down from 37 vessels on February 27. Some limited cargo movement resumed on Day 8 after Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologized to neighboring Gulf states for Iranian retaliatory strikes that damaged civilian infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, according to Al Jazeera.

Goldman Sachs warned that global oil prices could breach the $100 per barrel mark within days and reach $150 per barrel by the end of the month without a resolution to the shipping disruption, according to a report cited by the financial news aggregator ConflictLive.

🟥 WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

The connection between a clerical succession vote in Tehran and the price of diesel in Quezon City is direct.

Oil product prices in the Philippines are set to spike by P18 to P20 per liter on Tuesday, March 10, according to the Department of Energy as reported by the Inquirer and Philstar. If the full projected hike takes effect, diesel could reach P80 to P90 per liter.

The naming of a new supreme leader could prolong the conflict. If the Assembly confirms Mojtaba Khamenei and Israel follows through on its stated intention to target him, the war enters a phase where neither side has an obvious exit. That means the Strait of Hormuz stays disrupted. That means oil keeps climbing. That means every Filipino commuter, farmer, and fisherfolk pays the price.

Approximately 1.5 million land-based OFWs remain in the Middle East, according to DMW data. As of March 4, more than 1,400 have requested repatriation. A total of 2,012 have been served by the DMW, according to the Philippine News Agency. The House of Representatives has ordered a joint congressional briefing on OFW safety for March 11.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. departed Sunday for a three-day work visit to the United States, where he will address the UN General Assembly and call for peace in the Middle East, according to Philstar.

🟥 ICC PROSECUTION DEMANDS ANSWERS ON SIX FILIPINO LAWYERS IN DUTERTE DEFENSE TEAM

Six Filipino lawyers flew to The Hague last month for Rodrigo Duterte's confirmation of charges hearing. The ICC prosecution now wants to know exactly who they are to Duterte's legal defense.

In a six-page filing dated March 6, made public on Sunday, Deputy Prosecutor Mame Mandiaye Niang asked Pre-Trial Chamber I to compel Duterte's lead counsel Nicholas Kaufman to confirm in writing whether former Executive Secretary Salvador Medialdea, former Presidential Spokesperson Salvador Panelo, former Labor Secretary Silvestre Bello III, former Intellectual Property Office chief Martin Delgra III, Caesar Dulay, and Alfredo Lim Jr. are or are not part of the defense team.

The problem, as the prosecution frames it: contradictory signals.

🟥 THE MIXED MESSAGES

During the February 23 to 27 confirmation of charges hearings, Kaufman told the prosecution directly that the six lawyers were not part of Duterte's ICC defense team.

But his public statements told a different story.

In media interviews, Kaufman said his team was "ably assisted and supported by" the six lawyers. He told reporters that Delgra and Medialdea had been "working with us all along." Asked if the six would just be watching from the gallery, Kaufman responded: "Well yes, unfortunately they've arrived at the last minute, so it won't be possible to get them passes so they can actually sit inside the court."

Then Panelo, speaking separately to media, reportedly said Kaufman was scheduled to have a meeting with his "Filipino legal team" to discuss the confirmation hearing and potential strategies.

The prosecution's filing states that these statements "have created the impression that the six lawyers are members of Mr. Duterte's defence team before the International Criminal Court."

Before filing with the chamber, the prosecution emailed Kaufman on February 25 to ask for clarification. Kaufman did not respond.

🟥 THE SECURITY CONCERN

This is not a procedural technicality.

Under the ICC's rules, members of a defense team have access to highly sensitive confidential material. That includes the identities and personal details of prosecution witnesses and victims.

The prosecution cited Article 68 of the Rome Statute, which requires the court to "take appropriate measures to protect the safety, physical and psychological well-being, dignity and privacy of victims and witnesses."

In its filing, the prosecution explained: "This includes knowing which individuals work for Mr. Duterte's defence team, as these individuals have access to highly sensitive confidential information, including regarding Prosecution witnesses and victims."

The regulation at the center of this dispute is 23bis(2) of the ICC's Regulations, which designates Duterte's case as confidential to the defense, prosecution, and registry only.

If the six lawyers are not officially part of the defense team, they should have no access to any of that information. The prosecution wants that confirmed on the record.

🟥 WHAT THE PROSECUTION WANTS

The filing asks Pre-Trial Chamber I to order Kaufman to state in writing that the six lawyers are not members of Duterte's defense team and that they have not, and will not, receive any disclosure in this case. The prosecution also wants confirmation that they have not, and will not, have access to any non-public filings or any other non-public information. Finally, it asks Kaufman to confirm that the six are not entitled to legally privileged meetings or any other communications with Duterte at the ICC Detention Centre in Scheveningen.

If the defense refuses to provide that confirmation, the prosecution indicated it would seek to conduct risk assessments and potentially oppose their formal appointment.

🟥 WHO THE SIX LAWYERS ARE

The six are not random legal professionals. Four held senior positions in the Duterte administration during the exact period covered by the ICC charges.

Medialdea served as Executive Secretary from 2016 to 2022, the highest-ranking member of the Philippine cabinet. Panelo served as Chief Presidential Legal Counsel and Presidential Spokesperson. Bello served as Labor Secretary. Delgra headed the Intellectual Property Office.

All four served during the years when the ICC alleges Duterte ran a state policy that resulted in thousands of extrajudicial killings in the war on drugs. The government's own count puts at least 6,252 deaths in police anti-drug operations between July 2016 and May 2022. Human rights organizations estimate the total figure at over 30,000.

The Duterte camp has said the six were sent to The Hague "to ensure that he is properly represented by a Filipino legal team."

🟥 TIMELINE

Duterte was arrested on March 11, 2025 at Ninoy Aquino International Airport and transferred to The Hague on March 12. He faces three counts of the crime against humanity of murder and attempted murder. The confirmation of charges hearing ran from February 23 to 27, 2026. Pre-Trial Chamber I has not yet issued a decision on whether to confirm the charges and send the case to trial.

On the same day as this filing, March 6, the ICC Appeals Chamber separately upheld Duterte's continued detention, rejecting his second appeal for interim release.

🟥 IRAN WAR DAY 9: ISRAEL AND THE US STRIKE OIL INFRASTRUCTURE FOR THE FIRST TIME AS TEHRAN BURNS

Four oil storage facilities and one oil production transfer center in Tehran and surrounding Alborz province. That is what Israeli and American warplanes targeted late Saturday, according to Iran's Fars News Agency. The strikes ignited fires across multiple sites. Oil from the Shahran depot in western Tehran spilled onto the streets. At least four tanker drivers were killed.

It was the first time in nine days of war that the US-Israeli coalition went after Iran's oil infrastructure.

🟥 THE STRIKES

The targets, according to Iranian state media and Israeli military statements released on March 8: the Aghdasieh oil warehouse in northeastern Tehran, the area near the Tehran refinery in the south (the IRNA news agency reported the refinery itself was not damaged), the Shahran oil depot in western Tehran, and a depot in Karaj in Alborz province.

Israel confirmed it attacked "a number of fuel storage facilities in Tehran" that it said were being used "to support military operations," according to Al Jazeera.

Over the weekend alone, the Israeli military said it struck 300 targets in Iran, including missile storage sites and what it described as the underground bunker of Ali Khamenei beneath the regime's leadership complex. Since February 28, Israel says it has conducted approximately 3,400 strikes and dropped roughly 7,500 munitions, according to CBS News.

🟥 THE DEATH TOLL

According to Al Jazeera, citing Iran's health ministry, more than 1,300 people have been killed in Iran since February 28. Around 300 have been killed in Lebanon, where Israel has expanded operations against Hezbollah-linked targets. Approximately a dozen fatalities have been reported inside Israel. UNICEF has reported that 180 children have been killed across the conflict zones, according to CBS News.

The war began with the assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei on February 28, confirmed by Iranian state media on March 1. Iranian authorities are reportedly close to selecting his successor, with his son Mojtaba Khamenei considered a leading candidate. Israel has publicly stated it will target whoever is chosen.

🟥 THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil passes, has effectively gone dark. On March 3, only one ship transited the strait, compared to a typical daily volume of around 120 vessels, according to maritime traffic data corroborated by Reuters reporting.

Supertanker rates on the Middle East-to-China route surged to $423,736 per day, doubling from the previous Friday, according to LSEG data cited by Reuters. Qatar's LNG production halted. South Korea's maritime authority advised shippers to avoid the region entirely.

Iran says the strait remains "accessible" but warned it would target any US or Israeli vessels. Trump, when asked about reduced shipping, said it was a choice made by ships, according to Al Jazeera.

🟥 WHAT BOTH SIDES ARE SAYING

Trump demanded Iran's "unconditional surrender" and told reporters he has no timetable for ending the war: "Whatever it takes," according to CBS News.

Netanyahu said on Saturday that the US and Israel had gained "almost complete control of the airspace" over Tehran and that the campaign would continue "with full force," according to the BBC.

Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian publicly apologized Saturday on state television to Gulf neighbors for Iranian strikes that damaged civilian infrastructure across the region, according to the New York Times. Despite the apology, Iran continued launching missile waves at Israel on Sunday, with the IRGC calling it the latest salvo in "Operation True Promise," according to Al Jazeera.

🟥 THE PHILIPPINE FALLOUT

Diesel prices in the Philippines are projected to jump by P18 to P20 per liter on Tuesday, March 10, according to the Department of Energy as reported by the Inquirer and Philstar. If implemented in full, diesel could breach P80 to P90 per liter.

On Sunday, gas stations in Dagupan City and other areas reported running out of fuel after a wave of panic buying. The DOE deployed the Philippine National Police for nationwide inspections after some retailers raised prices by as much as P8 per liter ahead of schedule, according to the Inquirer. China, which supplies 30 percent of the Philippines' diesel imports, has suspended new fuel export contracts, according to the same outlet.

Approximately 800 Filipinos remain in Iran. Eleven were evacuated to Turkey on March 8 through a DFA-coordinated operation, according to Manila Bulletin and GMA News. A total of 1,979 Filipinos in nine Middle East countries have requested repatriation, but airspace closures continue to delay flights, according to the DFA.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. departed Sunday morning for a three-day work visit to the United States, where he will address the UN General Assembly and call for peace in the Middle East, according to Philstar and Manila Bulletin.

🟥 THE DAY BEFORE THE BIGGEST FUEL PRICE HIKE IN PHILIPPINE HISTORY, THE DAMAGE IS ALREADY SPREADING

Ferry companies are cutting routes. Shipping lines are slapping 20 percent surcharges on every ticket. A transport group is preparing a nationwide strike. And the fuel adjustment has not even taken effect yet.

Tuesday, March 10, is when oil companies are expected to implement the largest single-day fuel price increase the Philippines has ever seen. DOE minimum projections: diesel up P19 per liter, gasoline up P9, kerosene up P31. Higher industry estimates put diesel at P22 to P24, gasoline at P11 to P13, kerosene at P33 to P36.

Those are per-liter increases on top of already elevated prices. Not the new price. The increase alone.

Oil companies are expected to release their official price advisories today. Under DOE rules, companies must notify the department at least six hours before implementing any price increase.

🟥 WHAT IS ALREADY HAPPENING AT SEA

The shipping industry did not wait for Tuesday.

FastCat implemented new passenger and vehicle fares on March 6, citing a nearly 40 percent increase in global fuel costs. Roble Shipping added a temporary fuel surcharge starting March 9. Jomalia Shipping Corporation imposed a 20 percent fuel surcharge on all passenger tickets and freight rates, also effective March 9.

SeaCat went further. The Visayas-based operator suspended its Cebu-to-Catbalogan and Cebu-to-Baybay routes entirely — the cost of running them no longer made economic sense.

Montenegro Shipping Lines had already announced fare increases of 10 to 25 percent on all routes effective March 23. That announcement came before the DOE even released its latest minimum projections.

Routes that stopped running. Tickets that cost more. Cargo that costs more to move. Every island community connected by ferry is absorbing this before the biggest adjustment even lands.

🟥 ON THE STREETS

PISTON, the national federation of jeepney drivers and operators, is holding a rally today at East Avenue in Quezon City. The transport group has announced plans for a full nationwide strike by the end of March if fuel prices keep climbing and government relief remains insufficient.

PISTON's position: market speculation and profiteering are the real drivers behind the price spiral, not just the Iran conflict. The group has pointed to the Oil Deregulation Law as the structural weakness that leaves Filipino consumers exposed to every spike in the international market.

The LTFRB previously indicated it was considering provisional fare increases if diesel reached P60 per liter. Diesel has been above that threshold for weeks. On Tuesday, it could clear P80.

🟥 THE CONTINGENCY FUND PUSH

Senator Sherwin Gatchalian wants the government to stop waiting for the trigger.

The standard fuel subsidy program requires global oil prices to stay at or above $80 per barrel for a full month before subsidies kick in. Gatchalian has urged the executive branch to bypass that timeline and immediately tap President Marcos' P12 billion contingency fund for direct subsidies to PUV operators, ride-hailing drivers, tricycle drivers, farmers, and fishermen.

"If we delay that long, many individuals will suffer. Numerous drivers will be adversely affected," Gatchalian said on radio dwIZ.

He also warned the oil price surge could prevent the country from reaching its revised economic growth target of five to six percent — compounded by the spending contraction triggered by last year's flood control corruption scandal.

🟥 THE NUMBERS

DOE Oil Industry Management Bureau Director Rino Abad confirmed the minimum projections on Saturday. Those figures are based on five-day Mean of Platts Singapore trading data and do not include premiums or insurance costs tied to the Middle East conflict.

Industry sources project higher. Bombo Radyo La Union reported estimates of P22 to P24 for diesel, P11 to P13 for gasoline, P33 to P36 for kerosene. Manila Bulletin reported MOPS-based figures of P19.62 for diesel, P10.43 for gasoline, P24.92 for kerosene.

The DOE encouraged staggered implementation. Oil companies requested the full week to finalize calculations. According to Abad, if the increases are large, companies might stagger daily. If smaller, twice a week. The final decision rests with each company.

🟥 THE ENFORCEMENT GAP

Reports of premature price hikes surfaced across multiple provinces last week. In Tagum City, DOE Mindanao Field Office teams found a station that had raised diesel from P64.85 to P73.20 — an unauthorized P8.35 increase before the scheduled adjustment. Similar reports emerged from Laguna and Pampanga.

Manila 2nd district Representative Rolando Valeriano said fuel hoarding incidents are already being documented by citizens on social media. Former ACT Teachers party-list representative France Castro went further, accusing the government of hiding behind global market conditions while ignoring the Oil Deregulation Law — which she argued has allowed oil companies to coordinate increases rather than compete on pricing.

DOE Secretary Sharon Garin has maintained the line: "There is no need for panic buying. The country possesses a sufficient fuel supply."

That supply, however, now costs more to move, more to ship, and more to sell — and starting Tuesday, a lot more to pump.

WHEN THE LIGHTS GO OUT: HOW CINEMAS ARE DYING, FROM LOUIE’S THX TO MAGNOLIA

This reflection looks at the slow death of cinemas through one person’s memories, from Louie’s THX in Makati to Magnolia and SM Cabanatuan. It traces how streaming, high ticket prices, and changing habits quietly hollowed out what used to be a shared experience. Along the way, it asks what we lose when moviegoing becomes a rare luxury instead of a normal part of life.

Magnolia appears to be closing its cinema.

I read it the other day. Took me a second to process why it bothered me so much. It bothered me because it feels less like an isolated event and more like a pattern. The kind of pattern I've been watching unfold for decades.

I lived through the 80s, the 90s, the 2000s. I watched things rise, peak, and vanish. And every single time, it happened so gradually that by the time you noticed, it was already over.

WHEN CINEMAS WERE ALIVE

There was a time when cinemas had their own economy. Their own people.

I remember the lagaristas. Most people have probably forgotten the word, but if you grew up in the 80s, you knew what they were. They were the men on bicycles who carried film reels from one cinema to the next. Theaters didn't own the reels. They shared them. So a lagarista would race across Manila on a bike, hauling heavy canisters of film on a tight schedule so that one cinema could start its 3pm screening after the 1pm show ended at another theater down the road. The timing had to be exact. If the lagarista was late, the audience got angry, and would shout at the projectionist. That was the system. Analog. Human. Held together by a guy on a bike pedaling through traffic.

The practice was so much a part of the culture that Mel Chionglo made an entire movie about it in 2000. "Lagarista," starring a young Piolo Pascual as a film biker.

Then there were the hand-painted movie billboards. Not printed. Painted. Full-color, larger-than-life faces of Fernando Poe Jr. and Nora Aunor, Vilma Santos, and many more towering over Banawe, Recto, Legarda, or the corner of EDSA and Aurora Boulevard. 

Sagmit Advertising Inc. was the company behind most of them, founded in 1960 by Eduardo Sagmit, a former accountant at the 20th Century Fox Manila office. Vic Delotavo became the most well-known painter, doing posters for Regal, Viva, and Seiko Films starting in the early 80s.  The billboards were constantly painted over and reused, so almost no archive of them exists. Eventually, the work dried up. An entire art form, gone. Parang wala lang. 

And there were double features. Two movies for the price of one ticket. You'd walk in at noon and come out at five. You didn't even have to like the second movie. You watched it because it was there and you already had a seat.

Lagaristas on bikes. Hand-painted billboards. Double features on a Sunday afternoon. That was what the cinema world looked like when I was growing up. Every part of it is gone now.

THE FIRST THX CINEMA IN MANILA

This reflection brought me back to my favorite ciname - Loui's THX Cinema.

It was on Amorsolo Street in Makati, inside the Mile Long Compound. The Rufino family built it in 1994. They had a history with cinemas going way back, to the old Manila movie houses on Escolta and Rizal Avenue, places like Capitol Theater, the State, the Avenue, the Ever.

Louie’s THX was different from anything else we had. It was the most high-tech cinema in Makati at the time, THX-certified, with sound and seats that felt years ahead of the malls. It felt like the future.

The last movie I watched there was Brendan Fraser's The Mummy. 1999. I remember walking out of that theater thinking the experience couldn't get much better than that.

Then one day, it was gone. The cinema couldn't compete with mall-based multiplexes. It eventually shut down and sat empty. In 2014, a Japanese filmmaker named Toshihiko Uriu converted the space into TIU Theater, a performing arts venue for urban poor children. And then in 2017, the government took over the entire Mile Long complex after a long legal battle with Sunvar Realty. Everyone was told to vacate.

I'VE SEEN THIS BEFORE

I grew up watching technologies peak and disappear.

Pagers. Little black boxes clipped to your belt that made you feel important. Cellphones showed up and pagers were done. One-hour photo labs, there were over 7,600 of them in the US at their peak in 1993. All gone. Floppy disks, Walkmans, VHS tapes, PDAs. PDAs felt like the future until the iPhone buried them in 2007.

Every time, the cycle was the same. Something useful reaches its peak. Something more convenient comes along. The old thing doesn't die right away. It lingers. Becomes nostalgic. Then one day, you look up and realize nobody's used it in years.

Kodak invented the digital camera but couldn't save itself from digital photography. Blockbuster had the chance to buy Netflix and laughed it off. Nokia owned the mobile phone market and couldn't pivot fast enough when smartphones arrived.

The thing that replaces you doesn't even have to be better. It just has to be more convenient.

But I digress...

PHILIPPINE CINEMAS ARE BLEEDING

The numbers here at home are rough.

A study by the Film Development Council of the Philippines in collaboration with De La Salle University found that streaming services have become the primary platform for 67% of Filipinos. Only 21% still regularly go to cinemas.

That gap is massive. And it keeps growing.

FDCP Chair Jose Javier Reyes has been raising the alarm for a while now. He pointed out that the audience has shrunk dramatically. Only those in the A, B, and a small portion of the C socioeconomic classes still show up. The D and E classes have almost entirely stopped going. A single ticket costs around P500. A family of four needs at least P1,500 just for one movie, not counting transpo or a snack. The cinema has become a luxury.

Reyes also flagged something else. The concept of "return viewers," the people who used to watch a favorite movie two, three times in theaters, has nearly disappeared. People watch once, then wait for it to land on streaming.

Wala na yung dating excitement. Yung pipila ka pa sa ticket booth, yung mag-aaway kayo ng tropa mo kung saan uupo.

The pandemic made all of this worse. Three years of lockdowns changed habits for good. People got used to watching from home. K-dramas, foreign films, Netflix originals. The couch won.

In Alabang, Festival Mall's original cinemas opened in 1998 with six screens and eventually grew to ten. Now only two or three screens still operate on any given day. Commercenter's four-screen cinema in Filinvest City closed in 2025. That's fourteen screens gone from one city alone.

Even here in Cabanatuan, you can feel it. SM Cinema isn't immune. The slump is visible. Fewer people lining up, more empty seats on a Friday night. If a city like this is feeling it, the smaller towns don't stand a chance.

And now, Magnolia.

IT'S HAPPENING EVERYWHERE

This isn't just us.

AMC, the largest cinema chain in the world with over 850 locations, has closed 213 theaters in the past five years. In February, the company said it plans to keep closing more than it opens. Their Q4 2025 foot traffic dropped nearly 10%. From 62 million moviegoers down to 56.3 million. They posted a net loss of $632.4 million for 2025.

Globally, cinema ticket sales fell 8.8% in 2024. That's 500 million fewer tickets sold compared to 2023. Total attendance is now sitting at just 68% of 2019 levels. We haven't bounced back. We've plateaued. And the plateau is way below where we used to be.

In the US, the percentage of people who go to the movies at least once a month has dropped from 39% in 2019 to just 17% in 2025. Among those going less, 66% said cost was the main reason. Almost half said they'd rather wait and watch at home on a streaming service they already pay for.

Singapore had it rough in 2025. One journalist called it a "cinema-pocalypse." The Projector, an 11-year-old indie cinema beloved by film fans, suddenly shut down in August. Two weeks later, Cathay Cineplexes, with 87 years of history, ceased operations entirely. Filmgarde and WE Cinemas also closed. Four cinema operators gone in one country in roughly a year.

In the US, LOOK Dine-In Cinemas, which tried to reinvent the theater experience with food and premium seating, still had to close three LA locations in early 2026.

If even the reinvention model can't survive, what does that tell you?

WHAT STREAMING ACTUALLY REPLACED

Streaming didn't just replace cinemas. It replaced the reason to leave your house for entertainment.

When I was growing up, going to the movies was an event. You planned it. You got dressed. You argued with your friends about what to watch. The screen was massive, the sound was deafening, and for two hours, you were inside someone else's world.

Now you can get 80% of that experience on a 55-inch TV with a home theater and a Netflix subscription. And you don't have to wear pants.

The remaining 20%, the massive screen, the collective gasps, the feeling of strangers laughing together in the dark, that part can't be replicated. But the market has decided that 20% isn't worth the price of a ticket anymore.

Reyes admitted as much. The change was already happening as early as 2017 in the Philippines. The pandemic just sped up something that was already in motion.

And the younger generation, the ones who should be the future audience, grew up with content on their phones. They don't have the same attachment to the theater experience. Watching a movie on a laptop with earbuds is normal to them. They never experienced Louie's THX. They never sat through a double feature. They never saw a lagarista pull up on a bike with the next reel.

FOR CINEMA ONLY

I still watch movies in theaters. Every now and then. It's my small way of helping the industry, especially when something comes out that isn't another 
kabit-themed
 movie.

My daughter and I have this thing where we tag certain films as "for cinema only." The ones where the screen has to be big. The ones where the sound has to shake the seat. The ones that would lose something if you watched them on a laptop. We treat those like events, because they are.

The last movie I actually watched in a cinema wasn't even in the Philippines. I was in Canada, and my brother-in-law invited me to watch Avatar: Fire and Ash. On Boxing Day. The theater was packed. Every seat taken. I'll be honest, it had been a long time since I'd seen a cinema that full. I haven't seen that in the Philippines in a while, though I'll admit that might be because I'm confined to the province. Cabanatuan isn't exactly Metro Manila.

We watched it in 4DX. I'd never tried it before. It's this multi-sensory cinema technology developed by CJ 4DPlex. The seats move in sync with the action on screen. There's wind, water mist, scents, snow, lightning effects, vibrations. Over 20 different environmental effects built into the experience. They mostly use it for blockbusters and horror films, and Avatar was the perfect movie for it. When Pandora's oceans hit the screen, you felt the spray. When something flew overhead, the seat tilted with it.

That's a cinema movie. You don't watch that on a phone. You don't stream that on a laptop. You sit in that chair and you let the room take over.

But that's the thing. I had to be in another country to have that experience. And the fact that I remember it so clearly tells you how rare my cinema-going experience has become.

THE GRAVEYARD OF THINGS THAT PEAKED

I think about this more than I probably should.

The pager peaked in the 90s. The one-hour photo lab peaked in 93. VHS peaked just before DVD showed up. DVD peaked just before Blu-ray arrived. Blu-ray barely had time to breathe before streaming buried it. Each one, at its peak, felt permanent.

Cinemas felt permanent too. They've been around for over a century. They survived radio, television, home video, cable, and piracy. Every time, people said this would be the thing that killed cinemas. Every time, cinemas adapted.

But streaming might be different. Not because the content is better. Because the convenience gap is too wide. And because an entire generation built its viewing habits during a pandemic when theaters were closed for three years.

The AMC CEO talks about a strong 2026 lineup, with films like Spider-Man and the next Moana and Dune installments that should bring people back. And maybe they will. Blockbusters still draw crowds. But blockbusters are seasonal. The everyday moviegoer, the one who went on a random Tuesday night just because, that person is gone.

WHAT WE ACTUALLY LOSE

When a cinema closes, we lose more than a screen.

We lose a place where strangers shared something together. Where teenagers went on first dates. Where kids watched their first superhero movie and walked out feeling like they could do anything.

Sa Pilipinas, cinemas were one of the few places where class didn't matter as much. You bought a ticket, you sat in the dark, you watched the same movie as everyone else. The MMFF used to be described as "the people's festival," the one time ordinary workers would take their kids to the mall to see their idols on the big screen. That's fading.

Director Jun Robles Lana said it well. Cinema has gone from a shared national experience to a luxury for the middle class.

And once something becomes a luxury, it stops being part of the culture. It becomes niche. And niche things are the ones that end up next to pagers, floppy disks, and one-hour photo labs.

I don't know if cinemas will disappear completely. They probably won't. There will always be IMAX screens and 4DX rooms and premium experiences for those who can afford them. But the neighborhood cinema, the one inside the mall where you'd catch a movie on impulse, the one at Magnolia or Festival Mall or Commercenter, the SM Cinema here in Cabanatuan where the seats are half-empty on a weekend, those are the ones that are dying.

I watched Brendan Fraser fight a mummy in the first THX cinema in Manila. The building belongs to the government now. The screen is gone. The seats are gone. The lagaristas are gone. The hand-painted billboards are gone. The double features are gone.

And nobody noticed when it happened.

As someone who grew up watching movies in dark, crowded theaters, I feel a specific kind of grief watching this part of our lives fade. Call it a habit, a ritual, a small piece of culture we shared without thinking too hard about it. 

So while I still can, my quiet promise is simple: I’ll keep choosing the cinema when it matters, I’ll avoid piracy, and I’ll pay for the stories that deserve that giant screen. 

Tara, nood tayo ng sine. Bring your friends, your family, your workmates. Kung kaya pa, tulungan natin na mabuhay itong industriya.

🟥 DMW DECLARES PERSIAN GULF, STRAIT OF HORMUZ, AND GULF OF OMAN AS WARLIKE OPERATIONS AREAS — FILIPINO SEAFARERS CAN NOW LEGALLY REFUSE TO SAIL

On Saturday night, March 8, Migrant Workers Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac signed DMW Advisory No. 11, Series of 2026, officially designating the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas."

What that means in practical terms: any Filipino crew member assigned to a vessel operating in those waters now has the legal right to decline the assignment.

This is not a suggestion. It is a formal government advisory built on a resolution from the Warlike Operations Area Committee of the International Bargaining Forum, the global body that negotiates conditions for commercial seafarers. The IBF classification triggers specific provisions under international maritime labor agreements, including enhanced insurance coverage, additional compensation for war-risk sailing, and the crew's right of refusal.

🟥 THE SCOPE

The three designated zones cover virtually every major petroleum shipping route in the Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz alone accounts for roughly 20 to 21 percent of global daily oil consumption — about 21 million barrels per day transit through that chokepoint, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The Persian Gulf connects Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE. The Gulf of Oman sits between Iran and Oman, serving as the eastern entrance to the Strait.

Filipino seafarers are among the largest maritime labor forces in the world. The Philippines supplies an estimated 25 percent of the global merchant shipping workforce. According to the Philippine Overseas Labor Office, there were approximately 401,826 sea-based OFWs deployed in 2024. A significant number of those serve on oil tankers, bulk carriers, and container ships that routinely transit the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz.

🟥 WHAT THE ADVISORY ACTUALLY REQUIRES

In his video message Saturday evening, Cacdac laid it out directly: "Filipino crew members, especially ship owners employing Filipino personnel, should avoid operating in that region. If they must go there, they must grant Filipino crew members the autonomy to decline sailing and steer clear of these dangerous waters."

The advisory requires shipowners employing Filipino seafarers to do three things: first, inform crew members about the warlike operations designation before any transit through the affected zones; second, respect the right of any Filipino crew member to refuse sailing into those waters without penalty or contract termination; and third, comply with enhanced safety and compensation protocols mandated under the IBF framework.

The DMW also announced it would engage with shipowners, maritime labor groups, and international bodies to coordinate implementation. For Filipino seafarers already aboard vessels in or near the designated zones, the advisory's protections apply immediately.

🟥 WHY THIS IS DIFFERENT FROM THE GENERAL REPATRIATION

The Philippine government has already been managing repatriation of land-based OFWs from the Middle East since the US-Israel strikes on Iran began in late February. As of early March, over 1,100 OFWs had requested repatriation, and the DMW had also paused new deployments of land-based workers to the region.

But seafarers operate under a completely different framework. They are governed by international maritime labor conventions, not standard overseas employment contracts. Their vessels may be flagged under third-country registries — Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands — meaning the chain of legal authority runs through flag states, shipowners (often multinational), manning agencies in Manila, and international bargaining bodies.

The DMW advisory cuts through that layered jurisdictional tangle by invoking the IBF's warlike operations designation. It provides Filipino crew members with a recognized international legal basis to refuse assignment, backed by both Philippine government policy and global maritime labor agreements. Without this classification, a seafarer who refused to sail into the Gulf could face contract penalties or blacklisting by the manning agency.

🟥 THE TIMING

The advisory comes as Middle East hostilities continue to escalate. Since late February, the United States and Israel have conducted strikes against Iranian military and nuclear targets. Iran has responded with missile attacks on Israeli territory and US military installations in the Gulf states. Airspace closures have disrupted commercial aviation across the region. Oil prices have spiked globally, with the Philippines — which imports over 90 percent of its fuel — bracing for diesel price increases of up to P20 per liter this week.

For Filipino seafarers, the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz are not abstract geopolitical flashpoints. These are the waters they physically transit, sometimes multiple times per voyage, aboard vessels carrying the crude oil and petroleum products that fuel the global economy.

The last time the IBF classified a major waterway as a warlike operations area affecting Filipino seafarers at this scale was during the height of Somali piracy in the Gulf of Aden. That designation similarly triggered enhanced protections for crew members, including bonus pay, additional insurance, and the right to refuse transit.

He admitted he once lusted after Gabriela Women's Party Rep. Sarah Elago.

Topacio is a well-known figure in Philippine legal and political circles. He has represented clients tied to the Duterte administration and has built a media presence through his DWIZ radio program "Yes... Yes... Yo! Topacio."

He is a member of the Integrated Bar of the Philippines and is bound by the Code of Professional Responsibility and Accountability, the Supreme Court's ethical code for lawyers. That code requires members of the bar to avoid conduct that discredits the legal profession.

Three incidents involving three different men in three different institutional settings, all during Women's Month.

🟥 MARCOS ORDERS FOUR-DAY WORKWEEK FOR ALL GOVERNMENT OFFICES STARTING MARCH 9

Starting Sunday, every government office in the Philippines operates on a different schedule.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. signed Memorandum Circular 114 on Friday, directing all national government agencies, government-owned or controlled corporations, and local government units to adopt a four-day onsite work arrangement. The fifth day shifts to work-from-home or gets dropped entirely under a compressed workweek format.

The order takes effect March 9 and stays until Marcos personally revokes it.

Executive Secretary Ralph Recto signed the circular. The Civil Service Commission will issue the implementing guidelines for flexible work arrangements, attendance verification, and performance monitoring.

🟥 WHO IS EXEMPT

Police. Firefighters. Emergency responders. Any agency providing frontline public services.

The order explicitly carves out offices that handle emergency or critical functions. Everyone else in the executive branch falls under the new arrangement.

The policy does not apply to the private sector. George Barcelon, chairman emeritus of the Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry, said during a One News panel that some private companies might voluntarily follow suit, but acknowledged it would not be feasible across all industries.

That distinction has already drawn public frustration. Reactions on social media pointed out that private sector workers — particularly minimum wage earners who commute daily — face the same fuel cost pressure but receive no equivalent relief.

🟥 THE ENERGY CONSERVATION PACKAGE

The four-day workweek is one piece of a broader austerity directive.

MC 114 requires all government agencies to cut electricity consumption and fuel expenses by 10 to 20 percent. Every department must designate an energy conservation officer, cooperate with government energy audits, and submit monthly consumption data.

Non-essential government travel is now temporarily banned. That includes study tours, team-building events, and in-person meetings that could be conducted online. The circular specifies that travel without clear justification of urgency, operational necessity, or direct programmatic relevance counts as unnecessary.

🟥 THE TRIGGER

Marcos issued the directive in a video address on Friday, hours before the DOE confirmed the largest single-week fuel price hike in the country's deregulated history. Diesel was projected to rise P21 to P23 per liter on March 10. Gasoline P11 to P13. Kerosene P33 to P35.

The country imports over 90% of its oil from the Middle East. The ongoing US-Israel conflict with Iran has disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and sent global crude past $90 per barrel. The Department of Energy has warned prices will keep climbing for weeks.

Senator Sherwin Gatchalian had floated the four-day workweek idea earlier in the week as part of broader energy conservation proposals. Marcos initially said he would study the idea. By Friday, it was signed into policy.

🟥 ELECTRICITY RATES ARE NEXT

The same day Marcos signed MC 114, the Energy Regulatory Commission issued its own warning.

The ERC, in coordination with the Independent Electricity Market Operator of the Philippines, ran simulations on what rising international coal, oil, and liquefied natural gas prices would do to domestic power costs. Their finding: higher fuel costs could push prices in the Wholesale Electricity Spot Market upward as generators pass costs through their market offers.

The Philippines' energy mix leans heavily on coal and LNG. The ERC said the power sector remains particularly sensitive to global commodity price swings, and reminded distribution utilities and generation companies that existing market safeguards — including price caps and secondary price caps — remain in place.

For households, that means the government is bracing for a power bill increase on top of the pump price surge. No specific figures were released.

🟥 THE FUEL SUBSIDY QUESTION

Marcos mentioned during his video address that the government plans to introduce fuel subsidies to support sectors most affected by the price hikes. He did not provide details — no peso amount, no timeline, no mechanism for distribution.

He also warned business owners and traders against exploitation. Hoarding and price gouging, he said, remain illegal.

The government's fiscal room for fuel subsidies is constrained. The 2025 budget deficit came in at P1.58 trillion, overshooting the target by P60 billion. The BSP has signaled that inflation could breach the upper end of its 2 to 4 percent target if oil stays elevated. Capital Economics still expects further rate cuts, but Bangko Sentral Governor Eli Remolona has said $100 per barrel crude would change the calculation.

The four-day workweek costs nothing to implement. The fuel subsidies, whenever they arrive, will cost something the government may not have.

[UPDATE to Suntay-Anne Curtis Sexism Case] | March 7, 2026

Four days after Quezon City Rep. Bong Suntay publicly described a sexual desire for actress Anne Curtis on the floor of a congressional hearing, the legal and institutional walls are closing in from multiple directions simultaneously.

On Friday, March 6, nine women — including members of the World March of Women-Pilipinas (WMW-Pilipinas) and the Women's Legal and Human Rights Bureau — filed a formal complaint-affidavit at the Office of the Ombudsman against the lawmaker.

Jean Enriquez, national coordinator of WMW-Pilipinas, personally submitted the letter-complaint to Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla, calling for the investigation and imposition of appropriate penalties.

"It is evident that he is mimicking his idol, former president Rodrigo Duterte, whose sexist behavior and violence against women we have endured for a decade," Enriquez said. "Congressman Suntay has reignited trauma for many of us."

🟥 FOUR LAWS, ONE COMPLAINT

The complaint cites four legal frameworks Suntay allegedly violated.

The first is Republic Act 11313, the Safe Spaces Act, which prohibits and criminalizes sexist and degrading remarks in public spaces. The second is RA 9710, the Magna Carta of Women, which obligates government officials to not discriminate against women or allow violations of their rights. The third is RA 6713, the Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards for Public Officials.

The fourth is the Code of Professional Responsibility and Accountability, or CPRA — the Supreme Court's ethical code for lawyers. Because Suntay is a practicing lawyer, the complainants argued he violated the provision requiring lawyers to avoid scandalous behavior that discredits the legal profession.

The complainants called the remarks a "public crime." The Philippine Commission on Women (PCW) had already labeled them "a declaration of predatory desire directed at a woman, made openly, by a person who holds public trust and authority" — in a statement issued March 4 describing the words as "a public act of sexual objectification" bearing the hallmarks of gender-based sexual harassment under RA 11313.

🟥 THE ETHICS PROBE

Two days before the Ombudsman complaint was filed, the House of Representatives had already moved.

On the evening of Wednesday, March 5, at the close of plenary session, San Juan Representative Ysabel Maria Zamora moved to refer the matter to the House Committee on Ethics and Privileges. The motion authorized the committee to "thoroughly assess" Suntay's actions and statements, compel him to appear before the committee, and report findings and recommendations back to the full House.

No one objected. The motion passed.

House Committee on Women and Gender Equality Chair Ann Matibag had earlier delivered a privilege speech that laid the ground for the probe. "Women are not a joke, nor are they mere fantasies," Matibag said on the floor. "The dignity of women should never be treated as a source of ridicule within Congress."

Suntay said he was accepting the ethics complaint. "If they want to file an ethics complaint, if they want to judge me, the person that I am, based from one particular statement, I respect it," he said.

He maintained his remarks carried no malicious intent. "The analogy may have been in poor taste and taken out of context. But if you read the context, there was nothing malicious in it."

🟥 THE GASLIGHTING ACCUSATION

The nine complainants at the Ombudsman explicitly challenged Suntay's characterization of his own apology.

His apology — issued March 4 — expressed regret only for those who were "offended," while defending the remarks as an analogy and insisting there was no malice. The complainants called this "gaslighting." They argued that framing "inappropriateness" as subjective — something that depends on how an individual received it — dismisses the real harm caused to women and re-traumatizes survivors of gender-based violence.

"The apology and the removal of his remarks from the committee records do not absolve him of responsibility," Jelen Paclarin of the Women's Legal and Human Rights Bureau said.

🟥 ILOILO WEIGHS IN, ANNE CURTIS HOLDS

The League of Municipalities of the Philippines — Iloilo Chapter (LMP-Iloilo) released a statement on Saturday condemning the remarks and calling public officials to account.

"Comments that could belittle, objectify, or undermine the dignity of women contradict the principles of gender equality and respectful dialogue that our institutions strive to uphold," the group said.

The statement underscored the timing — the controversy erupted during National Women's Month.

Anne Curtis, for her part, had already rejected Suntay's apology on March 7 with a public statement. "I do not accept your non-apology," she wrote. She confirmed she is seeking legal advice — a decision that may add a separate private action to the growing stack of institutional complaints already in motion.

Suntay now faces exposure on three parallel tracks: a criminal complaint before the Ombudsman, a formal ethics investigation by the House, and the possibility of legal action by Curtis herself.

He authored Quezon City's Gender and Development Code and its anti-harassment ordinance — a fact he has cited repeatedly in his own defense.

🟥 SENATOR JINGGOY ESTRADA ASKS GILAS WOMEN PROSPECT ELIZABETH MEANS IF SHE HAS A FILIPINO BOYFRIEND DURING NATURALIZATION HEARING — DEFENDS IT AS "NOTHING MALICIOUS"

March 7, 2026

The hearing on March 3 was supposed to be about citizenship.

Elizabeth Jeanette Means, an American basketball forward and Gilas Pilipinas Women's prospect, appeared before the Senate Committee on Justice and Human Rights to advance her naturalization under Senate Bill 1594. The committee — chaired by Senator Francis "Kiko" Pangilinan — was examining whether she genuinely intended to make the Philippines her permanent home.

Senator Jinggoy Estrada had a different line of questioning.

After asking Means where she lived and what Filipino food she liked, Estrada asked: "Do you have a Filipino boyfriend?"

Means replied, "Not yet po." Estrada then added: "Maybe Senator Pangilinan might want to apply." He followed with: "Sorry kay Sharon."

Pangilinan, who is married to actress Sharon Cuneta, responded with a laugh: "Magagalit yung commander."

The exchange was livestreamed. It spread quickly.

🟥 WHO ELIZABETH MEANS IS

Means is a 6-foot forward from Folsom, California. She completed her NCAA Division II career at Westminster University in Utah, then spent her first professional season in Ecuador. Coach Anton Altamirano of the Gilas Women's program approached her agent and brought her to the Philippines, where she has been based since late 2025.

At the Senate hearing alongside her was Maodo Malick Diouf — the UAAP Most Valuable Player from the University of the Philippines — who is seeking naturalization for Gilas Pilipinas 3x3. Both athletes expressed that they already felt at home in the Philippines during the hearing.

Means' case was filed under Senate Bill 1594, authored by Senator Pia Cayetano. The Senate committee is one step in a formal legislative process that also requires House passage and presidential approval.

She was there to answer questions about her commitment to the country. The question she was asked instead was about her romantic life.

🟥 THE BACKLASH

Social media reaction was swift and pointed.

Filmmaker Jun Lana posted on X: "When will our leaders realize that their casual sexism is a national embarrassment?"

Ceej Tantengco-Malolos, founder of sports advocacy group Go Hard Girls, called it inappropriate and out of place — a senator asking an athlete about her personal life during what should have been a formal assessment of her commitment to Philippine citizenship. The question, she said, was irrelevant to anything the committee was tasked to determine.

Other online reactions called the remark rude and disgusting, with several users pointing out that Estrada knew Pangilinan was married — and to one of the most recognizable women in Philippine entertainment history.

One widely shared comment put it plainly: "Every day, may entry sa Women's Month ang mga public officials, na para bang may challenge for the most insensitive, sexist politician."

🟥 ESTRADA'S STATEMENT

On Friday evening, Estrada broke his silence through a formal statement.

"It may not be widely known, but Senator Pangilinan's wife, Ms. Sharon Cuneta, is a dear friend of mine, and our friendship goes back many years — long before I entered politics," he said.

He offered a procedural explanation for the boyfriend question: "Part of the Committee's responsibility is to understand whether applicants intend to truly make the Philippines their home — whether by settling here permanently or even establishing a family in the future."

"There was absolutely nothing malicious in the question," he added. "It was asked in a light and conversational manner during the proceedings."

He described the exchange as "friendly banter" directed at his colleague Pangilinan, not at Means.

🟥 THE TIMING

This is the second incident in the first week of Women's Month involving a male lawmaker making remarks about a woman during formal proceedings.

On the same day as the Means hearing — March 3 — Quezon City Representative Bong Suntay made headlines for describing a sexual fantasy about actress Anne Curtis during a House committee hearing on the Sara Duterte impeachment complaints. Suntay's remarks drew a formal complaint to the Office of the Ombudsman, a House ethics referral, and a public statement from Anne Curtis rejecting his non-apology.

Both incidents occurred inside formal legislative chambers. Both involved a male lawmaker directing unsolicited personal or sexualized commentary at a woman. Both happened in the same week of March — which the Philippine government officially designates as Women's Month.

Suntay's remarks were eventually struck from the record by a 13-2 committee vote. In the Estrada case, no committee objection was recorded. No formal complaint has been filed as of Saturday.

🟥 WHO JINGGOY ESTRADA IS

Estrada is a sitting senator and the son of former President Joseph Estrada. He is a member of the Senate Committee on Justice and Human Rights — the same committee that handled Means' naturalization hearing.

As of this writing, he is also under a precautionary hold departure order issued February 10 by Manila Regional Trial Court Branch 52. The order was issued in connection with a plunder case filed by the National Bureau of Investigation over alleged irregularities in P355 million worth of flood control projects in Bulacan. His co-respondents include former DPWH Secretary Manuel Bonoan and three other former DPWH officials. The charges include plunder under Republic Act 7080, violations of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, and direct bribery. Estrada has denied all allegations.

His counter-affidavit in the case was due March 9, 2026.
